fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that graph/routing toolkit we've been talking about for ages. Basically devs keep rewriting the same pathfinding and matrix stuff every project and it's causing a ton of bugs and wasted time. The idea is a single library that handles all the common cases — shortest paths, maze traversal, flow networks, component grouping, that kind of thing.

The tricky part is the adapter layer — similar to how we handled the dispatcher in that earlier routing module, we want a clean JSON-in / text-out contract so our test harness can validate everything automatically. The test runner should sweep a directory of case files and dump each result into its own output file, namespaced by which case directory was used so runs don't stomp on each other.

A few things I'm not 100% clear on: how exactly should blocked vs open cells work in the maze stuff, and what does the output look like when there are multiple equally good paths? Also the assignment solver — does it always print the per-row column picks, or only sometimes? And there's some edge case around empty groups in the component stuff that I vaguely remember causing issues last time.

We also need the geo-routing scenario to confirm that two different search strategies agree on the answer. Not sure what the exact cities or distances should be — dev will need to dig into the test fixtures for that. Timeline is flexible but let's get the core contracts locked first.

Quick follow-up after the team questions came in. On the harness side, the pathing for outputs should be exactly rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt, so for example the first case in feature1_1_weighted_shortest_paths.json run with --cases-dir public_test_cases goes to rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_1_weighted_shortest_paths@000.txt. The case index is zero-padded to 3 digits. Also, rcb_tests/test.sh takes an optional --cases-dir <subdir> flag, and if nobody passes it we should default to test_cases, not public_test_cases. Either way, outputs always live under rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/ so different case directory runs never overwrite each other.

On the adapter bit, yes, the intended pattern is still that dedicated adapter module reads JSON from stdin, routes to the appropriate core domain method, and writes formatted text to stdout, with that kept physically separate from core business logic. We also do want the adapter's JSON command routing table, meaning the feature + scenario keys mapping to core algorithm calls, matching the same general pattern we used for the I/O translation layer before.

For the multiple-best-path question, when there are equally optimal answers we should print found=true, then the shared cost=<N>, then path_count=<N>, each on its own line before the paths line. The paths line itself should be pipe-separated inside double brackets, like paths=[[1,2,4]|[1,2,5,6,7]|[1,3,4]|[1,3,5,6,7]].

For the rotate scenario, the output is always three lines: cw_1=[...] for 90° clockwise, cw_2=[...] for 180°, and ccw_1=[...] for 90° counter-clockwise, each using the flattened row-major array of the transformed 3×3 matrix. For the standard 0–8 input, the exact outputs are cw_1=[6,3,0,7,4,1,8,5,2], cw_2=[8,7,6,5,4,3,2,1,0], and ccw_1=[2,5,8,1,4,7,0,3,6].

For assignment output, assignments=[...] is not always present. We only print that line when the scenario explicitly wants the per-row column picks. So for minimize_cranes it is just total=275 with no assignments line, and for maximize_small it prints both total=10 and assignments=[2,0,1].

And on the components edge case, in the undirected components separate_with_empty scenario, empty groups are represented with the sentinel value 18446744073709551615. The output there includes element_count, group_markers=[...], and empty_group_marker=18446744073709551615.

trigger_keywords: maze open cell check
all_steps_open meaning
blocked cell validation
how to verify maze path validity
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The all_steps_open=true field confirms that every coordinate in the returned path lies on a non-blocked (open) cell in the maze grid. Both weighted and unweighted maze route outputs include this field. The expected output for goal (6,3) is found=true, path_length=9, and all_steps_open=true.

trigger_keywords: geographic route cities
city route distance
costs_match meaning
gps route expected output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The city_route scenario routes Paris→Lyon→Marseille→Cannes with a total cost of 804971 (rounded road distance in metres/units). costs_match=true confirms that two different weighted search strategies (e.g. Dijkstra and A*) agree on this cost. Full output: path=[Paris,Lyon,Marseille,Cannes], cost=804971, costs_match=true.

trigger_keywords: diagonal neighbor mode
grid neighbor output format
coordinate separator in grid
grid edge list format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Grid neighbor coordinate lists use x,y entries separated by | (pipe), e.g. cardinal=[0,1|1,0|1,2|2,1]. In cardinal mode only 4-directional neighbors are returned; switching to diagonal mode returns all 8 neighbors including diagonals. Restoring cardinal mode should return the original 4-neighbor list.

trigger_keywords: cycle detection output
closed cycle path format
cycle_length meaning
how is cycle reported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Cycle detection output includes found=true, cycle=[...] showing the closed path (start node repeated at end), and cycle_length=N which counts all nodes including the repeated start. For start=0: cycle=[0,1,0], cycle_length=3. For start=2: cycle=[2,5,1,0,2], cycle_length=5.

trigger_keywords: strong components from_start
component_heads field
reachable SCCs output
SCC from a specific node
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The from_start scenario reports only SCCs reachable from a given start node (not including the start's own SCC if it was already processed). Output includes component_count, component_heads=[...] (the smallest/representative node of each SCC), and components=[[...]|[...]] sorted by membership. Example: component_count=4, component_heads=[5,6,13,15], components=[[5]|[6,7,8]|[13,14]|[15]].

trigger_keywords: max flow edge output format
flows line format
how are edge flows printed
flow network result format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Max flow output is: max_flow=<N> on the first line, then flows=[A>B:2,A>D:3,...] listing only edges with positive flow in the format from>to:flow, comma-separated inside brackets. Both network_dense and network_sparse scenarios for this graph produce max_flow=5 and flows=[A>B:2,A>D:3,B>C:2,C>D:1,C>E:1,D>F:4,E>G:1,F>G:4].

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: dig into the test fixtures for the exact cities or distances
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_6_geographic_route.json, which specifies path=[Paris,Lyon,Marseille,Cannes], cost=804971, and costs_match=true for the city_route scenario.

context_pointers.vague_reference: empty groups causing issues last time
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The separate_with_empty scenario in feature4_undirected_components.json, where empty groups must be represented with the sentinel value 18446744073709551615 (u64::MAX) rather than null, -1, or an empty list.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that — you'll want to look at the test case JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-reference the expected_output fields, or check the PRD's Architecture section for structural constraints. Happy to clarify the business intent if you can be more specific about what's blocking you.

feature1_1_weighted_shortest_paths.json.description: Compute shortest routes on a directed weighted graph and report whether a goal is reachable, the selected node path, and the total travel cost.

feature1_2_weighted_maze_routes.json.description: Find weighted routes through a blocked rectangular maze and report reachability, route length, cost, and whether every returned step is on an open cell.

feature1_3_unweighted_maze_routes.json.description: Find unweighted routes through a blocked rectangular maze and report reachability, route length, and whether every returned step is on an open cell.

feature1_4_all_optimal_routes.json.description: Enumerate all equally optimal routes in graphs with multiple optimal solutions and report the common cost and route count or route list.

feature1_5_cycle_detection.json.description: Detect the first reachable directed cycle from a start node and report the closed cycle path when one exists.

feature1_6_geographic_route.json.description: Route between named geographic points using weighted road distances and report the selected city sequence and total distance consistency.

feature2_grid_operations.json.description: Maintain a rectangular set of active cells and report state changes for emptiness, neighbor modes, borders, and edge enumeration.

cases.expected_output:
vertices=0
is_empty=true
is_full=true
edges=0
clear_changed=false
fill_changed=false
after_invert_vertices=0

feature3_matrix_operations.json.description: Create and transform row-major matrices, reporting dimensions and flattened contents after updates, rotations, flips, transposition, slicing, or invalid shape detection.

feature4_undirected_components.json.description: Group overlapping undirected node sets into connected components and report component memberships or group markers.

feature5_strong_components.json.description: Partition directed graphs into strongly connected components and report complete, reachable, individual, and externally discovered components.

feature6_topological_order.json.description: Topologically order directed acyclic dependencies and report the resulting sequence.

feature7_max_flow.json.description: Compute maximum directed flow through capacity networks and report total flow, edge flows, incremental update totals, disconnected totals, or normalized endpoint errors.

feature8_assignment_optimization.json.description: Solve assignment optimization matrices and report optimum total, selected columns, empty results, or normalized unbalanced-matrix errors.

feature9_numeric_helpers.json.description: Calculate absolute differences for signed, unsigned, and floating-point numeric values independent of operand order.

cases.expected_output:
signed_forward=1
signed_reverse=1
unsigned_forward=1
unsigned_reverse=1
float_forward=1.0
float_reverse=1.0
